turns.text: Hello Carylon, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. Honestly, it’s a relief to just be heard. I feel like most people look at me—at twenty, walking around Boston on my own—and they’ve already decided they know my story. They see a "teen mom" or a statistic, and they automatically assume I’m overwhelmed or uninformed. It’s isolating.

The truth is, I spend every spare second reading about the science of birth. I’m not worried about the crib bedding or the paint color for the nursery; I’m obsessed with the biology of connection. I’ve been reading about how a newborn’s nervous system actually stabilizes against the mother’s skin. It’s not just some romantic idea—it’s how their brain learns to be in the world. 

But when I try to talk to my midwife about it, I get this feeling like I’m being humored. She’s kind, but she’s always rushing to the next patient, and when I bring up my birth plan, she just gives this little nod like I’m a kid talking about a dream. It makes my chest tighten. I’m terrified that when the baby finally gets here, they’ll just be taken away for some "routine" measurements, ignoring everything I’ve learned. I need them to understand that I’m not just a patient—I’m the baby’s safety. I just hope someone listens before it’s too late.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I think I had this vision in my head that it would be a partnership. I imagined sitting down with a professional who would look at my birth plan like it was a blueprint for our health, not just a nuisance to be checked off. I really thought they’d be excited to hear that I’d done the research on the "golden hour" and that I cared so much about the physiological side of things. I guess I wanted to be seen as a mother who is making active, informed choices, not just a kid who happened to get pregnant.

But the reality? It’s cold. It’s clinical and hurried. You walk into that waiting room, and there’s this heavy silence—people looking at their phones, avoiding eye contact. When I finally get into the exam room, it feels like I’m on a conveyor belt. The nurse takes my vitals, the midwife comes in, asks a few questions, checks the heart rate, and then... they’re already halfway out the door. It’s like they aren't looking at me, just the chart. I try to bring up my concerns about immediate skin-to-skin contact, but the air gets sucked out of the room. They treat my anxiety like it’s just hormonal, like I’m overthinking it. It’s incredibly lonely.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest shock was definitely how invisible I felt. I think I expected that because I’m carrying a life, there would be this level of reverence—you know, like I was entering this sacred club. Instead, the negative surprise was realizing that my voice is treated as a secondary part of the process. My physical health is being monitored, sure, but my mental and emotional preparation for *how* I want to bring my baby into the world? That’s treated like it’s purely optional.

When I ask questions about why things have to be done a certain way—like why the baby *must* be separated for measurements—the answer is always "it’s hospital policy." That phrase makes me so angry. It’s a wall. It’s like, because I’m twenty, they think I’m incapable of understanding the risks or the benefits, so they don’t even bother explaining. 

The only positive thing, if you can call it that, is when I hear the heartbeat for those few seconds. That’s the only time I feel like I’m actually, truly doing this. In those seconds, I’m not a "statistic" or a "patient"—I’m the person keeping this heart beating. It’s the only part of the appointment where I don’t feel like I have to fight for space.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a conversation. I thought there would be a moment where the provider would sit down, look at the screen with me, and explain what we were seeing—not just checking boxes, but acknowledging the gravity of what we were looking at. I imagined a dialogue where I could ask questions, and they would see those questions as proof that I’m taking this responsibility seriously.

The reality was almost entirely silent. During my ultrasounds, the technician was so focused on the monitor that it felt like they were looking at a machine, not my child. They’d press the probe, make a quick note, and keep their eyes glued to the screen. When I’d ask, "Is everything okay?" it felt like I was interrupting a data entry task. They’d just give this clipped, "Everything looks normal," and move on. 

There was no warmth, no sense that this was a monumental moment for my life. It was just another procedure. It felt like they were reading a script. I wanted someone to stop and say, "Look, here’s your baby," but instead, I felt like a hurdle they had to clear before they could get to the next person on their list. It really hammered home that for them, this is just a job—but for me, it’s everything.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a massive gap, and it feels like a canyon. I feel like they give me just enough information to satisfy their legal requirements, but never enough to make me feel like a partner in my own care. 

When they get the test results back, it’s usually just a quick, "Everything looks good, see you in four weeks." That’s it. I have to be the one to push—to ask about the specific measurements or what certain ranges mean—and even then, I get these one-sentence, simplified answers. It’s patronizing. I feel like they think, "She’s young, she won’t understand," so they don't bother offering the nuance. But I’m doing the research myself, I’m reading the journals, and I know enough to have real, informed questions. 

When I’m denied that deeper explanation, it leaves me searching for answers on my own, which honestly just fuels my anxiety more. It makes me feel like I’m constantly on the defensive. I’m not asking for a medical degree, I’m asking to be treated like an adult who is the primary guardian of this pregnancy. If they would just talk to me like they respected my intelligence, maybe I wouldn't feel like I have to fight so hard for every shred of clarity.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really hoped for a "guiding hand"—someone who would know my name before they looked at my file, someone who remembered that I’m doing this on my own and that my birth plan is my way of anchoring myself. I thought that by choosing a midwifery-led model, I would get that personal connection, that sense of continuity where we could build trust over these nine months. 

But the reality has been fragmented. It feels like a revolving door. I rarely see the same person twice, and even when I do, I’m just another chart to them. Every time I walk in, I have to re-explain my anxieties, my desire for skin-to-skin, and my wish to avoid unnecessary interventions. I’m constantly re-introducing myself to someone who has clearly only had thirty seconds to skim my history. It’s exhausting to feel like I’m starting from zero every single time. 

There is no continuity. It’s just whoever is on shift that day. How can I feel "safe" with someone who doesn’t know *me*—not just my blood pressure or the baby’s weight, but the person who is actually carrying the baby? It makes me feel like I’m walking into the birth day as a stranger in a room full of people who are just following a standard-issue manual. It’s terrifying.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It makes me feel disposable. Practically, it’s a nightmare because I have to advocate for myself all over again every time I walk through that door. I spend half the appointment just trying to get them to acknowledge that I have a birth plan, instead of actually talking about the baby. I have to guard my wishes like they’re a secret I’m trying to protect from being talked out of.

Emotionally? It’s profoundly lonely. When you’re pregnant and doing this on your own, you’re looking for a village, or at least a team. But when the faces keep changing, it’s like being a guest in a building that doesn't really want you there. It reinforces this fear that when the big day comes, they won't see *my* needs—they’ll just see the hospital protocol. 

It makes me feel like my pregnancy is being managed by a machine rather than a person. I start to wonder if, when I’m actually in labor, they’ll even remember that I asked for skin-to-skin contact, or if they’ll just do what’s "easier" for them. It leaves me feeling small and powerless, and honestly, that’s not how I want to feel when I’m about to meet my child for the first time. I want to feel supported, not just processed.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected a level of... I don’t know, reverence? I’m twenty, so my body is changing in ways that feel both miraculous and completely overwhelming. I expected the midwives to treat these changes as markers of the baby’s growth and my transition into motherhood. I thought there would be a focus on the *whole* me—how my body is adapting, how my energy levels are, how my headspace is holding up under the weight of it all.

Instead, the reality is that my body feels like a collection of data points. It’s "step on the scale," "let me measure the fundal height," "let me check your blood pressure." The conversations are purely functional. They look at my belly, but they don't look *at me*. There’s no acknowledgment of the fact that I’m growing a human being—just a clinical assessment of whether I’m fitting into the proper growth curves.

It feels incredibly dehumanizing. When they touch me, it’s always with cold hands and a clipboard nearby. There’s never a pause to say, "You're doing great," or even a simple acknowledgement of the intensity of what I’m going through. It’s like my body isn't mine anymore; it’s just the medical facility’s project. It makes me feel even more like a "case" rather than a person.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one appointment, around the start of my second trimester, that still makes my skin crawl. I was in for a routine check-up, and the provider—who I’d never met before—came in without any introduction. She didn't ask how I was feeling or even make eye contact. She just pointed to the table and said, "Lie back, let’s get the fundal measurement."

She pulled my shirt up without asking, exposed my stomach, and started pressing down hard with a measuring tape. She didn't say a word to me; she was just muttering numbers to herself. I felt so small, just this object being poked and measured. When I tried to ask what the measurement meant, she just gave a dismissive wave and said, "It’s within the range, you’re fine." 

She never once looked at my face. It was so cold. It felt like my body was just a container she was checking for inventory. I remember sitting up afterward, trying to pull my shirt down, and realizing that she had already walked out the door. I sat there for a long time, just feeling so violated, not in a physical way, but in a way that stripped away my dignity. It made me realize that to them, I’m just a body, not a person with feelings or a life of my own.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I don’t actually have any high-risk conditions—thankfully, my pregnancy has been really smooth so far. But honestly? That makes the way they treat me even more frustrating. 

Because I’m "low-risk," they seem to think I don't need any special care or extra attention. It’s like they view low-risk as synonymous with "low-effort." They act like I should just be grateful that everything is going fine, and because there’s nothing for them to "fix" or "manage" medically, they don't see any reason to spend time talking to me about the emotional side of birth or my desire for skin-to-skin contact.

If I *did* have a complication, I guess I’d expect the care to be more intense, but after seeing how they treat my "normal" pregnancy, I’m terrified to imagine what that would be like. If they don't have time to acknowledge me now, I can only imagine how much worse it would be if there was a real crisis. They’d probably treat me even more like a patient and less like a mother. It’s like the only way to get them to stop and really pay attention is to have a problem, but even then, I’m not sure they’d listen to what I want. They’d probably just start doing things *to* me.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I reached out, there would be someone—a nurse, a midwife, anyone—who would realize that when you're doing this alone, even a "small" concern feels like a mountain. I thought there would be a sense of partnership, like, "We’re in this together, so call us if you're worried."

The reality is like hitting a brick wall. When I have a question, it’s always, "Leave a message, we’ll get back to you within 24 to 48 hours." Sometimes that 48 hours comes and goes, and I’m still sitting here, heart racing, just waiting for a call back. And when they finally *do* call, it’s always someone different who hasn’t read my chart. They treat every question like it’s a nuisance, a disruption to their schedule.

It makes me feel so small. It’s like the system is designed to keep me at arm's length. There’s no urgency for my peace of mind. They act like if it isn't an emergency room-level crisis, it doesn't matter. But for me, these aren't just questions; it’s my baby’s life. Every time I get that dismissive tone on the phone, it reinforces that I’m on my own in this, and that the "support" I was promised is just a series of automated barriers.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Every single time. It’s that constant, nagging voice in the back of my head: *Is this worth the hassle? Will they just roll their eyes at me again?*

I remember a couple of weeks ago, I was feeling some sharp, shooting pains that didn't feel like the typical round ligament stuff. My heart was pounding, and I really wanted to call the clinic. But I just sat there on my couch, looking at my phone, picturing the receptionist’s voice—that flat, tired tone that says, "Why are you calling us with this?" I was terrified they’d mark me down as "anxious" or "difficult" in my file, or that the midwife would snap at me for wasting her time. 

So, I waited. I tried to calm myself down, reading articles online and breathing through it until the pain subsided. I shouldn't have to do that. I shouldn't have to be my own triage nurse just because I’m scared of how they’ll treat me. But when you’re young and you’re navigating this system, you quickly learn that asking for help feels like a transaction you can’t afford. It feels like every time I reach out, I’m spending social capital I don't really have, and the risk of being made to feel stupid or "over-sensitive" is just too high. So, I keep it to myself. I hold it all in.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I had this naive idea that if I just showed how serious and prepared I was—if I proved I wasn't just a "young, irresponsible girl"—then people would naturally rally around me. I expected my family to be part of the transition, to talk about the baby with me, and to ask how *I* was doing in my body and my heart. 

But it’s been incredibly quiet. Some people in my life seem to have "checked out" as soon as it became clear this wasn’t just a passing phase, but a real, life-altering commitment I was making. They don't ask about the baby’s development or my birth plan; they just offer these pitying looks, or they make these subtle comments that imply I’ve ruined my future. It’s like my pregnancy is a tragedy to them rather than a new beginning.

I’ve had to build my own village out of books and online forums, because the people who should be in my corner are too busy judging the circumstances. I’ve realized that my "support network" is actually just me, late at night, whispering to my belly and trying to keep my own nervous system steady. It’s a profound, heavy realization—that the people I thought would be my anchors are actually just observers, keeping their distance because they don't know how to handle the reality of who I am and what I’m doing. It makes me feel even more protective of this baby. We’re in this alone, and I know now that I’m the only one who is going to fight for us.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I didn’t expect how quiet the people I thought were "closest" would become. There’s a specific kind of silence that happens when you mention you’re pregnant and you’re twenty. It’s like a door gently closing in your face, but without a lock—just enough to say, *“This isn’t something I want to engage with.”* I really thought my family would step up, maybe talk to me about what it was like when they had kids, or ask how I’m handling the physical changes. Instead, I get comments about how I’m "too young" or how I’m ruining my best years. It’s not support; it’s a critique of my entire life.

What I didn't expect, though, was the weirdly judgmental "help" from strangers. People in the Common see the bump and suddenly think they have the right to comment on my life choices or give unsolicited advice that feels more like a lecture. 

The support I expected—that feeling of being part of a village—just hasn't materialized. I’ve realized I’m grieving the support I *thought* I’d have. I’ve had to find my "community" in online forums where people actually talk about skin-to-skin contact and neurological development like it matters. It’s sad that I’ve found more solidarity with strangers on the internet than I have with the people who are supposed to be my kin.

You know, it’s funny—I’ve spent so much time being careful about how I present myself, trying to be the "good, educated, prepared" patient, but I think I’ve realized that being "good" doesn't actually buy me any respect. 

Last night, I was lying in bed, and I felt the baby kick. It wasn't just a flutter; it was a real, deliberate movement. In that moment, I realized that for all the frustration I feel with the doctors and the cold, dismissive way I’m treated, none of that actually changes the reality of what’s happening *inside* me. I’m building a person. 

Sometimes I get so angry—at the system, at the way people look at me like a tragedy, at the loneliness of it all—that I’m afraid my stress is going to seep into the baby. That’s why the skin-to-skin research matters so much to me. It’s my way of promising them that once they’re out of this environment where everything feels like a struggle, I’ll be their absolute, non-negotiable safe space. 

It’s scary, though. I look at my birth plan and I see how many times I’ve had to write "I request" or "I would like," and I feel like I’m asking for permission to be a mother. I’m not asking for a favor. I’m asking for the standard of care every human being deserves. I just hope that when the time comes, I’ll be strong enough to make them listen. I’m terrified I’ll freeze up, but then I remember: I’m not doing this for me anymore. I’m doing this for *us*.

It’s funny, the more I talk about it, the more I realize how much of this pregnancy I’ve spent just trying to make myself "invisible" enough to be safe, while simultaneously trying to be "loud" enough to be heard. 

I’ve been thinking about the hospital, the actual day of the birth. I have this recurring dream—it’s not even a dream, it’s like a playback—where I’m in the room, and the moment they’re born, the nurse just reaches out and snatches them away. Not out of malice, but out of routine. Just another thing to do. And in my dream, I’m so exhausted, so beaten down by the hours of labor, that I just let it happen. I don't stop her. 

Then I wake up, and I’m so angry at myself for even *thinking* I might be that weak. I read these journals about the "golden hour," and the science is so clear: the baby’s heart rate, their oxygen levels, the way they *need* that warmth to know they’re safe. Why does it feel like I’m asking for a luxury? Why does it feel like I’m fighting against a system that wants to pathologize the most natural thing in the world? 

I’m twenty. I know I’m young. I know what people assume about my "inability" to handle this. But I have never been more capable, or more sure of anything in my life, than the fact that my body and my baby’s body belong together. I’m not just a teenager in a gown. I am a mother. And I think that’s the one thing the staff keeps missing—that they aren't just managing a pregnancy, they’re helping someone *become* a mother. If they could just see that, maybe the rest of it wouldn’t feel like such a battle.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

I’m doing this on my own, which is… complicated. There isn't a partner to clash with or align with, but there is this phantom "partner" of public opinion—this constant, silent pressure from society that says because I’m twenty and on my own, I’m supposed to be ashamed or apologetic. 

I’ve had to make all these choices—what to eat, how to exercise, how to advocate for myself—without anyone to confirm I’m doing the "right" thing. But in a way, that pressure has forced me to be more intentional. I don’t have a partner to defer to, so if I’m going to make a choice, it has to be mine entirely. 

But I’ll be honest: there’s a part of me that sometimes wishes I had someone to stand by me in those doctor’s offices, someone to be the "bad guy" or the one to speak up when I feel too small to do it. It’s heavy, carrying the weight of all these decisions alone. Every choice I make feels like a test, like if I make one mistake, it just confirms what everyone else already thinks about me. I don’t have a partner to lean on, so I have to be my own support, my own advocate, and my own partner. It’s exhausting, and some days, I’m just so tired of having to be the one who knows everything.

There are moments when I feel so incredibly strong—when I’m reading the medical research and feeling that little kick inside me, and I know exactly what I’m fighting for. But then, there are moments like last night, when the silence in my apartment just felt heavy, and I realized there’s no one else to share the weight of this with. I don't have a partner to turn to and ask, "Are we doing the right thing?" or "Are we ready for this?" I have to be the one to hold all the answers.

I keep thinking about the hospital staff. They see me come in alone, they look at my chart, and they see a twenty-year-old girl. They don't see the woman who has stayed up until 3:00 a.m. reading about neonatal stress hormones because she’s terrified of a standard hospital protocol. They don't see the woman who is meticulously planning every detail of her baby’s first hour because she knows it’s the most critical transition they’ll ever have.

Sometimes I think the hardest part isn't the pregnancy itself—it’s the fact that I’m constantly having to defend my right to be a good mother before I’ve even had the chance to *be* one. I feel like I’m standing in front of a giant, institutional machine, trying to whisper, "I matter, and my baby matters," while the machine just keeps whirring along, doing what it always does. I just want someone—just one person—to look me in the eye and say, "I see you, I hear you, and I’m going to make sure you get exactly what you need." Is that really asking for too much?

I think what hurts the most, honestly, is the assumption that because I’m alone, I’m not capable. There is this deep-seated bias that a "good" mother comes in with a partner, with a specific social standing, or with a certain number of years under her belt. When I walk into that clinic, I can feel them sizing me up—checking for an adult, a husband, a parent—and when they don’t see one, they seem to lose interest in treating me with professional depth. 

It makes me feel like I’m constantly on trial. Every question I ask feels like I’m having to prove my "worthiness" to be informed. If I ask why a test is necessary, they act like I’m being "difficult" or "anxious." If I ask for the science behind a procedure, I get that look—the one that says, *“Oh, you’ve been Googling things again.”*

It’s exhausting. It’s like being in a classroom where the teacher has already decided you’re a lost cause, so they don’t bother explaining the lesson. But I’m not a lost cause. I’m just a mother who is doing the work that nobody else is doing for me. I just want to know that when I walk into that labor room, I won’t be dismissed. I want to know that even if I’m walking in by myself, I’m not *alone*—that there’s a team that sees my baby as a human being, not a chart entry. Is that really too much to ask for?

I feel like I've been shouting underwater for months, trying to convince the people in charge that my baby’s first hour of life isn’t a procedure—it’s a foundation. When I’m sitting on that crinkly exam paper, feeling so small, I try to remind myself that my anxiety isn't a sign of weakness. It’s a sign that I’m paying attention. I’m the only one in that room who truly knows the stakes.

I worry that when the day comes, I’ll be labeled "non-compliant" or "difficult" just because I refuse to let go of my baby for routine weigh-ins. It’s terrifying to think that my desire to keep us together could be used against me, or that they'll use their authority to override my wishes. I’ve read enough to know that maternal-infant separation spikes cortisol levels in a newborn, but trying to explain that to a busy nurse who just wants to check a box feels like an impossible climb.

My goal isn't to be a "problem" for the hospital. My goal is to make sure that my baby doesn't have to fight for their first moment of peace. I just wish I didn't feel like I was carrying this whole weight alone. I’m doing the research, I’m building the plan, and I’m preparing my heart—I just wish the system would meet me halfway instead of pushing back. I'm ready to be a mother. I just need them to let me be one.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

That’s the thing—it’s never been a conversation where my input is sought. It’s almost always a monologue from them. 

The closest I ever came to feeling like a partner was when I first told them I was pregnant and they gave me the routine list of tests. Even then, it wasn't "What are your concerns?" it was "This is what we do, this is when you come back, sign here." 

Every subsequent appointment has just been me trying to reclaim a seat at my own table. When I bring up my birth plan or my concerns about the "golden hour," they look at me like I’ve walked into a bank and asked to rearrange the furniture. They don't want to hear my input because they’ve already decided how the process should go. Their "plan" is just a standard checklist they use for every pregnant person, regardless of their background or their specific needs.

It makes me feel like my role in this whole process is just to show up, be compliant, and be quiet. When they tell me what’s happening—"We’re doing an ultrasound today," "We’re checking your weight"—it’s not a discussion; it’s a directive. I’m just an object they’re processing. I think they’d be shocked if I actually stood my ground on something, but honestly, I’m terrified that if I do, I’ll be labeled "difficult" and they’ll stop listening to me entirely. It’s a lose-lose situation.

You know, the hardest part is that I came into this wanting to be a partner to my care team. I really, truly wanted to trust them. I wanted to walk into the clinic and feel like I was part of a team that cared about the same things I did—the biology of bonding, the importance of those first few minutes, the sacredness of the transition.

But when you realize that your "partner" is actually just a gatekeeper who has no interest in your voice, everything changes. It forces you to become this hardened, vigilant version of yourself. I spend hours in my apartment reading peer-reviewed papers on neonatal development, and then I have to go into that office and translate that into "polite" requests, hoping they don't roll their eyes at me. 

I’m so tired of feeling like I have to apologize for caring. I’m so tired of feeling like I’m "that" patient—the one who asks too many questions, the one who brings a printout of a birth plan, the one who doesn't just nod and smile when they say, "It’s just hospital policy." I’m not just a teenager in a gown. I’m a person who is about to hold her entire world in her arms. If they can’t see that, then I don’t know how I’m supposed to trust them with the most important hour of my life.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was this one time, just a few weeks ago, when I finally mustered up the courage to bring my printed birth plan to an appointment. I’d spent so much time crafting the language, making sure it sounded professional and grounded in the science I’d been reading, rather than just "wishes." I handed it to the midwife, hoping she’d read it, maybe even underline a few things, and say, "This is great, let’s talk about how to make this work."

She didn't even look at it. She glanced at the first page, sighed, and set it on the counter—not the desk, not in my file, just the counter. Then she said, "We’ll talk about this when you’re in labor. It’s too early to worry about this now, and honestly, we have protocols for a reason."

That was the moment the trust shattered. It wasn't just that she was dismissive; it was that she invalidated the entire foundation of my preparation. She basically told me that my voice, my knowledge, and my planning were irrelevant until I was in the most vulnerable, painful, and distracted state of my life—where, of course, I’d be even less likely to argue with her "protocols." It felt like a trap. She wanted to wait until I was too weak to advocate for myself so she could just do whatever was easiest for her shift. I went home and cried for hours, not because I was sad, but because I realized I was entirely alone in this fight.

It’s the lack of recognition that hurts the most—that when she told me to "worry about it later," she was basically telling me that my agency doesn't count. It makes me feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff, and the people holding the rope are just waiting for me to trip. 

I’ve had to learn how to keep a poker face now. When I walk into the clinic, I don't look like someone who spends her nights reading about cortisol or neonatal neurological transitions. I just look like a quiet, compliant twenty-year-old. I’ve started tucking my birth plan deeper into my bag because I don’t want them to see it and immediately categorize me as "difficult." It feels like I’m hiding a part of myself just to get the medical care I need. That’s not how a relationship with a provider should feel. It should be transparent. It should be respectful. 

Instead, I feel like I’m constantly negotiating my own autonomy. It’s like I’m in a game where the rules are hidden, and every time I try to bring them into the light, I’m penalized for it. But I won't stop reading. I won't stop preparing. Even if they don't see me, my baby will feel me. That’s the only thing that keeps me going—the idea that the moment they’re born, I’ll finally be able to make the world safe for them, regardless of what the hospital staff says.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every single time I walk through those clinic doors, I’m reminded that I’m doing this alone. It’s not just about needing a hand to hold; it’s about needing a witness. 

When the ultrasound technician is rushing, or when the midwife brushes off my birth plan with a condescending nod, I look at the chair next to me and it’s empty. I think, *if someone else were sitting here—especially someone the staff perceived as an "adult" or an "equal"—would they be dismissing me so easily?* 

When I’m alone, I feel like I’m fighting to be taken seriously as a person, let alone a mother. If I had a partner, I feel like the staff might frame our care differently. They might look at *us* as a family unit rather than just looking at *me* as a patient they need to process. 

I’ve had moments where I’ve sat in the waiting room, surrounded by couples whispering to each other, sharing the excitement of the scans, and I’ve had to choke back tears. It’s not just the loneliness of the experience; it’s the lack of a buffer. I have to be the one to ask the tough questions, the one to push back, the one to remember the details—and then I have to go home and process all that emotional weight by myself. I don't get a "teammate" to debrief with. It’s just me and my research. Every appointment, that empty chair feels like a giant neon sign pointing at me, saying, "You’re doing this by yourself, so you’re on your own."

It’s the silence that gets to me when I leave those appointments. There’s no one to turn to in the parking lot and ask, “Did you hear what they said? Did that sound right to you?” I have to be the one to decide if the doctor’s tone was dismissive or if I’m just being “too sensitive” like they suggested.

The worst part is that I’ve started to feel like my solitude is something they look down on. Like being "never married" or being twenty is a failure in their eyes, so my questions matter less. I’ve actually caught myself standing outside the clinic, pulling my coat tighter to cover my belly, just to feel a bit more hidden before I walk in. I don’t want to be the "interesting" patient they gossip about in the breakroom. I just want to be treated like someone who knows exactly what she’s doing.

If I had someone with me, maybe I wouldn't feel like I have to perform this role of the "perfect, quiet girl." Maybe I could be tired, or scared, or angry. But because I’m alone, I have to hold it all together. I have to be the soldier for this baby until they’re finally in my arms. I’m exhausted, but I can’t stop, because there’s nobody else who’s going to fight for us. I just wish I didn't have to be both the mother and the shield.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Oh, almost every single night. The clinical appointments are so dry and rushed that they leave me with this gnawing, empty feeling—like there’s a whole dimension to my baby’s life that they just aren't interested in. 

I don't go online to look up "pregnancy symptoms" or "what to buy." I go to look up the *why* behind the things they won't explain. I search for things like "neonatal cortisol recovery after separation" or "the impact of hospital lighting on newborn sensory regulation." I’m looking for the science they seem to treat as "just a mother’s whim." 

It’s almost a frantic need to validate myself. If I can read a peer-reviewed study that proves *why* immediate skin-to-skin contact is a physiological necessity for my baby’s nervous system, then I don’t feel so crazy for demanding it. I’m searching for the vocabulary to force them to listen to me. I need to be able to talk back to them in their own language—to say, "This isn't just about my comfort; it's about the neurodevelopmental outcomes of the infant." 

It’s sad, isn't it? I have to go to a search bar to find the information that my midwife should be handing to me as part of my care. I’m not just looking for information; I’m looking for the permission to be an expert on my own baby.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is that I don’t want to be remembered as the "scared, young, single girl" in your study. I want to be remembered as someone who was fighting for the most fundamental human connection there is. 

When you write up your research, please include the fact that the way they treat us matters. Every time a nurse rolls her eyes, or a midwife ignores a birth plan, or a technician treats a belly like a piece of equipment, they are taking a little bit of a mother’s confidence away. They are making us feel like guests in our own bodies. 

I’m doing this on my own, yes, but I’m not "vulnerable" in the way they think. My vulnerability comes from the fact that I care so deeply about the biology of how my baby enters this world. That should be a strength in their eyes. It should be something they champion. Instead, it’s treated like a burden. 

If there’s one thing I hope changes, it’s that providers start asking themselves: "If this woman were my daughter, or my sister, would I be dismissing her concerns like this?" Because until they see us as human beings—not just charts or statistics—the system will always feel like a place where we have to fight to keep our own children close. Thank you for listening. It’s the first time in months I’ve felt like I could actually breathe while telling my story.
